questions.stem: The law of demand states that, other things being equal, when the price of a good rises the quantity demanded will do what?
questions.explanation: The law of demand says price and quantity demanded move in opposite directions, ceteris paribus. A higher price reduces the quantity buyers are willing and able to purchase, giving a downward sloping demand curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The law of supply states that, other things being equal, a higher price for a good will normally cause the quantity supplied to do what?
questions.explanation: Supply slopes upward: a higher price makes production more profitable and encourages firms to offer more for sale, so price and quantity supplied move in the same direction.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A change in the price of a good itself causes which of the following?
options: A movement along the demand curve (a change in quantity demanded)
A change in the determinants of demand
A shift of the whole demand curve
questions.explanation: A change in the good's own price is shown as a movement along a fixed demand curve, called a change in quantity demanded. Only a change in some other determinant shifts the whole curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following would cause the demand curve for a normal good to shift to the right?
options: A fall in the price of the good itself
A rise in the price of the good itself
An increase in the number of firms producing it
questions.explanation: For a normal good, higher income raises demand at every price, shifting the demand curve right. A change in the good's own price moves along the curve, and the number of firms affects supply, not demand.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Two goods are substitutes if a rise in the price of one leads to which effect on the demand for the other?
options: An increase in demand for the other good
A decrease in demand for the other good
questions.explanation: Substitutes are goods used in place of one another, such as tea and coffee. If coffee gets dearer, some buyers switch to tea, so the demand for tea rises. Demand for a substitute moves in the same direction as the other good's price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Petrol and cars are complements. A large fall in the price of petrol is most likely to do which of the following to the demand for cars?
questions.explanation: Complements are consumed together. Cheaper petrol lowers the overall cost of running a car, so more people buy cars. Demand for a complement moves in the opposite direction to the other good's price.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is most likely to shift the supply curve of wheat to the left (a decrease in supply)?
options: A fall in the price of wheat
A new subsidy paid to wheat growers
A drought that destroys part of the harvest
A fall in the wage paid to farm workers
questions.explanation: A drought reduces the amount that can be supplied at each price, shifting supply left. Lower input costs or a subsidy would shift supply right, and a price change is a movement along the supply curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Market equilibrium occurs at the price and quantity where which condition holds?
options: Price is at its highest possible level
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is where the demand and supply curves intersect, so the quantity buyers want to buy equals the quantity sellers want to sell. There is then no tendency for price to change.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the market price is set above the equilibrium price, what will tend to result?
questions.explanation: Above equilibrium, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, creating a surplus. Sellers with unsold stock tend to cut the price, pushing the market back towards equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the market price is set below the equilibrium price, what will tend to result?
options: A surplus that grows over time
questions.explanation: Below equilibrium, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, so there is a shortage. Competition among buyers tends to bid the price up towards equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Holding supply fixed, an increase in demand (a rightward shift of the demand curve) will do what to the equilibrium price and quantity?
questions.explanation: With supply unchanged, a rightward shift of demand creates a temporary shortage at the old price. Price is bid up and firms move up the supply curve, so both equilibrium price and quantity rise.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Holding demand fixed, an increase in supply (a rightward shift of the supply curve) will do what to the equilibrium price and quantity?
questions.explanation: A rightward shift of supply creates a surplus at the old price, so price falls. As price falls, buyers move down the demand curve and quantity demanded rises, giving a lower price and higher quantity at the new equilibrium.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A price ceiling (maximum price) set below the equilibrium price is most likely to cause which of the following?
questions.explanation: An effective price ceiling holds price below equilibrium, where quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. The result is a persistent shortage, often leading to queues, rationing, or black markets.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A price floor (minimum price) set above the equilibrium price, such as a minimum wage above the market clearing wage, tends to create which of the following?
options: A surplus of the good or labour
A shortage of the good or labour
questions.explanation: A binding price floor keeps price above equilibrium, where quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded. In a labour market a wage floor above equilibrium produces a surplus of labour, that is unemployment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An improvement in the technology used to produce a good is best shown on a demand and supply diagram as which of the following?
options: A movement up the supply curve
questions.explanation: Better technology lowers production costs, so firms can supply more at each price. This shifts the supply curve to the right, tending to lower the equilibrium price and raise the quantity traded.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: For an inferior good, a rise in consumer incomes will do what to demand?
questions.explanation: An inferior good is one people buy less of as they get richer, switching to preferred alternatives. So higher income shifts the demand curve for an inferior good to the left.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If buyers expect the price of a good to rise sharply next month, the most likely effect on current demand is which of the following?
options: Current demand increases as buyers purchase now
questions.explanation: Expectations are a determinant of demand. If buyers think prices will rise, many bring purchases forward, so the present demand curve shifts to the right.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When both demand and supply increase (both curves shift right) by similar amounts, what can be said with certainty about the new equilibrium?
options: Quantity rises, but the effect on price is uncertain
questions.explanation: Two rightward shifts both raise the equilibrium quantity, so quantity clearly rises. The two shifts push price in opposite directions, so without knowing their relative sizes the change in price is indeterminate.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The demand schedule and demand curve are usually drawn for a given period assuming which of the following is held constant?
options: Income, tastes, and the prices of related goods
Only the price of the good
questions.explanation: A demand curve shows how quantity demanded varies with the good's own price while holding the other determinants (income, tastes, prices of substitutes and complements, expectations) constant. Relaxing any of those shifts the whole curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In a competitive market, the role of the price mechanism in clearing a shortage is best described as which of the following?
options: Price falls, reducing quantity supplied further
Price rises, which reduces quantity demanded and raises quantity supplied
questions.explanation: A shortage means quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. The resulting upward pressure on price reduces quantity demanded (movement up the demand curve) and increases quantity supplied (movement up the supply curve) until the market clears.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An increase in an indirect tax (such as a sales tax) levied on producers of a good is shown as which of the following?
options: A movement along the demand curve only
questions.explanation: A tax on producers raises their costs at every output level, so they supply less at each price. The supply curve shifts left (equivalently, upward by the amount of the tax), tending to raise price and lower quantity.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which statement about a normal good is correct?
options: It has a vertical demand curve
Its demand falls when consumer income rises
Its demand does not respond to income
Its demand rises when consumer income rises
questions.explanation: A normal good is defined by a positive relationship between income and demand: as income rises, demand for the good increases. This contrasts with an inferior good, whose demand falls as income rises.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A fall in the price of a key raw material used by a firm will most likely do which of the following to the market for the firm's product?
options: Shift supply left, raising price and lowering quantity
Have no effect because raw materials are not part of demand
Shift demand right, raising price and quantity
Shift supply right, lowering price and raising quantity
questions.explanation: Cheaper inputs lower production costs, so firms supply more at each price and the supply curve shifts right. With demand unchanged this lowers the equilibrium price and raises the equilibrium quantity.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Black markets (illegal trading above the legal price) most commonly arise as a consequence of which policy?
options: An effective price ceiling that creates a shortage
questions.explanation: When a binding price ceiling holds the price below equilibrium, the resulting shortage means some buyers cannot get the good legally. This creates an incentive for illegal sales above the controlled price, a black market.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A shift of the demand curve to the left, holding supply constant, will do what to equilibrium price and quantity?
questions.explanation: A leftward shift of demand creates a surplus at the old price, so price is bid down. As price falls, firms move down the supply curve, so both the equilibrium price and quantity fall.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following best distinguishes a 'change in demand' from a 'change in quantity demanded'?
options: A change in quantity demanded shifts the curve; a change in demand moves along it
They are two names for the same thing
Both refer only to changes in supply
A change in demand is a shift of the curve; a change in quantity demanded is a movement along it
questions.explanation: A change in quantity demanded is caused by the good's own price and is a movement along a fixed curve. A change in demand is caused by a non price determinant (income, tastes, related prices) and shifts the entire curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
